topic: Why Resting Meat Makes It Juicier

packaging.title: Why Cutting Steak Too Soon Floods Your Board
altTitles: The Real Reason Resting Steak Makes It Juicier
Why Steak Bleeds When You Cut It Early
packaging.description: A food science look at what really happens inside a steak as it rests, why slicing too soon spills the juice onto your cutting board, and how muscle proteins reabsorb water as the meat cools.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A just-cut steak with a glistening pool of juice escaping onto the board, a timer ticking beside it

packaging.hookPromise: Cut your steak the second it leaves the pan and you'll watch a tablespoon of flavor run onto the board — here's the invisible reason waiting five minutes saves it.
openLoops: Where exactly does the juice go while the steak 'rests' — it doesn't just sit there?
Why does a hot steak push out more liquid than a slightly cooled one?
Is resting actually about the juice, or is the temperature doing something we can't see?
packaging.payoff: The juice isn't 'settling' — as the meat cools below ~120°F, contracted muscle proteins relax and physically reabsorb the water they squeezed out during cooking, so resting literally pulls liquid back into the fibers instead of onto your board.
packaging.whyItWorks: Nearly everyone has cut a steak early and seen the juice spill, so the curiosity gap is universal and the answer is a genuinely counterintuitive bit of protein science.
